---
title: "How ledgr became faster than Backtrader (while doing more correctness work)"
subtitle: "The v0.1.8.7 optimization round, explained"
author: "ledgr maintainer review"
date: "2026-05-29"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 9
---

## TL;DR

Before this release cycle, ledgr was about **2.74× slower than Backtrader** on a matched 500-symbol × 1,260-day SMA crossover backtest. After this cycle, with TTR-backed indicators, **ledgr is about 1.94× *faster* than Backtrader** and about 3.3× faster than quantstrat on the same workload, same host.

That speedup is real, but it understates the story. ledgr *also* writes a sealed, deterministic, event-sourced ledger to DuckDB on every pulse — work that Backtrader and quantstrat don't do. So ledgr now wins the speed race **while doing more correctness work per bar** than its peers. The reproducibility USP didn't have to be paid for; the previous slowness was overhead, not a correctness tax.

The single biggest contribution came from one specific fix: we replaced an event-buffer over-allocation pattern that had been quietly eating about 72% of the loop's R-level time. Everything else in the cycle — legacy removal, dependency simplification, representation cleanup, reconstruction cleanup — was either preparation for that fix or polish around it.

This document explains, in concrete but accessible terms, what was wrong before each change, what was done, what was measured, and what general R-and-package-development lessons we'd recommend carrying forward.

## The shape of the cycle

The work was organized into nine batches with explicit dependencies, so that each measured improvement could be attributed to the right change rather than being lost in compounding effects.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    B0[Batch 0<br/>Scope alignment]
    B1[Batch 1<br/>Legacy execution cleanup]
    B2[Batch 2<br/>Dependencies & function strategies]
    B3[Batch 3 - B0<br/>Event buffer rewrite<br/><b>the headline win</b>]
    B4[Batch 4<br/>Collapse determinism wrapper]
    B5[Batch 5<br/>Representation & setup cleanup]
    B6[Batch 6<br/>Reconstruction read-back cleanup]
    B7[Batch 7<br/>Run-artifact materialization policy]
    B8[Batch 8<br/>Post-lane benchmark & attribution]
    B9[Batch 9<br/>Release gate]

    B0 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B2 --> B4 --> B6
    B3 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7 --> B8 --> B9

    style B3 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

Three things to notice about the dependency graph:

1. **Legacy cleanup goes first** (Batch 1). The optimizer's job is much easier when it doesn't have to preserve obsolete invariants. A surprising amount of the reconstruction performance came from being allowed to *delete* code rather than make it faster.
2. **The determinism gate (Batch 4) is a precondition for any value-bearing use of the `collapse` package.** Until that wrapper exists, no reconstruction code may use `collapse::rowbind` or `fcumsum`. The gate is a contract, not a feature.
3. **B0 (Batch 3) and Lane R (Batch 5) are measured separately.** This was non-negotiable: their wins are next to each other in the profile, and a casual compounding multiplication would have made each contribution unverifiable.

## The headline win: removing the event-buffer rock (Batch 3 / B0)

This is the single most important change in the cycle and the one most worth understanding in detail. Almost everything else in the cycle is small by comparison.

### What ledgr's event buffer actually does

When ledgr runs a backtest, every fill — every order execution, every CASHFLOW, every semantic rejection — is recorded as a row in a sealed event ledger. That ledger is hash-verified, deterministic, and forms the basis for replay, inspection, and the reproducibility guarantees ledgr exists to provide. It lives in DuckDB.

Writing every event directly to DuckDB per pulse would be wasteful (lots of small round-trips). So ledgr buffers events in memory across a number of pulses, then flushes them in batches to DuckDB.

The buffer is a set of typed R vectors — one vector per column of the ledger schema (`event_id`, `ts_utc`, `instrument_id`, `side`, `qty`, `price`, `fee`, `meta_json`, `event_seq`, and a few others).

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    Pulse[Per-pulse loop] --> Strategy[Strategy callback]
    Strategy --> Fills[Fill events]
    Fills --> Buffer["In-memory buffer<br/>(11 typed R vectors)"]
    Buffer -- flush --> DuckDB[(Sealed DuckDB ledger)]
```

### The problem: worst-case preallocation

Before this cycle, the buffer was allocated at run start to its **worst-case capacity**: `n_instruments × n_pulses`. For a 500-symbol × 1,260-day backtest, that meant each of the eleven columns was a vector of **630,000 slots**, ready to receive events.

For every event the runner did roughly this:

```r
i <- state$n + 1L
state$cols$event_id[[i]]      <- new_event_id
state$cols$ts_utc[[i]]        <- new_ts_utc
state$cols$instrument_id[[i]] <- new_instrument_id
# ...one assignment per column...
state$n <- i
```

That looks innocuous. It is not.

In R, when you write `vec[[i]] <- value` on a vector that has a reference count greater than 1 — which happens routinely from closure capture, ambient references, and the way the output handler holds the buffer object — R **copies the entire vector** before modifying the one cell. That's a fundamental property of R's copy-on-modify semantics applied to refcount-elevated vectors.

So writing **one** event required copying ~630,000 values, eleven times (once per column). Writing **N** events required N such writes, with the column copy cost the same for each one. The total cost was **O(fills²)** in the number of events — quadratic, not linear.

| Metric | Pre-B0 (worst-case prealloc) |
|---|---|
| Initial buffer capacity | 630,000 slots × 11 columns |
| Cost per event written | ~7 million value copies |
| Total cost for 13,000 events | ~90 billion value copies |
| Share of loop R time (LDG-2457 profile) | **72.43%** |
| Buffer self-time, 500×1,260 SMA workload | ~137 seconds |

That last row is the operational headline. About **half** of a 295-second backtest wall was the buffer-write copy cost.

### The fix: grow-by-doubling

We replaced worst-case preallocation with the standard amortized-O(n) growth pattern that compiled languages use for dynamic arrays (`std::vector` in C++, `Vec` in Rust):

- **Initial capacity**: `min(1024, max_events)`.
- **On capacity exceeded**: double the buffer (1,024 → 2,048 → 4,096 → ...).
- **Hard cap** at the original worst-case ceiling — so the runtime still fails loudly if a strategy ever produces too many events, but never silently grows past the contract.

The implementation lives as a shared helper in `R/fold-core.R` and is used by both the durable handler and the in-memory sweep handler:

```r
ledgr_event_buffer_next_capacity <- function(current, required, max_events) {
  if (required > max_events) {
    rlang::abort("Ledger event buffer exceeded the run's maximum capacity.",
                 class = "ledgr_event_buffer_capacity_exceeded")
  }
  if (required <= current) return(current)
  capacity <- max(current, 1024L)
  while (capacity < required) capacity <- ceiling(capacity * 2)
  min(max_events, capacity)
}
```

The columns are reallocated and existing rows copied in bulk only when the capacity boundary is crossed. Per-event writes themselves go into the *current* (small) buffer, not a giant one. Total copy cost is amortized linear, not quadratic.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    subgraph After["AFTER: grow-by-doubling"]
        A1["Start with 1,024 slots"]
        A2["Fill up to 1,024"]
        A3["Double to 2,048 when needed"]
        A4["..."]
        A5["Hard-cap at worst-case ceiling"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    end
    subgraph Before["BEFORE: worst-case prealloc"]
        B1["Allocate 630,000 slots up front"]
        B2["Every per-event write<br/>copies all 630,000 values"]
        B1 --> B2
    end
    style Before fill:#fff0f0
    style After fill:#f0fff0
```

### The measurement

We re-profiled the same workload, on the same host, on current source.

| Metric | Pre-B0 | Post-B0 |
|---:|---:|---:|
| Buffer-write self-time | 137.08 s | 1.50 s |
| Share of sampled R time | **72.43%** | **3.49%** |

That's the **mechanism evidence**: the buffer's share of R-level time dropped by a factor of about twenty. The wall-time comparison across the two runs crosses a local CPU power-profile change so isn't directly comparable end-to-end, but the within-run profile *share* is apples-to-apples and is the honest claim.

This single change is what made every later "we're competitive with Backtrader" measurement possible. Everything else in the cycle is small by comparison.

### Why this is a generally useful R lesson

R is unusual among modern languages in that "modifying" a vector with an index assignment doesn't always modify in place — when the vector's reference count is elevated (which it often is in production code, in ways that aren't obvious from reading the code), the assignment copies the entire vector. Profiling tools don't always make this visible: the time shows up as ambient cost, not as a "copy" call.

So in R, **over-allocation is not "harmless caution," it's a write-cost trap**. The instinct to preallocate to worst-case size to "avoid reallocations later" comes from languages with stable in-place writes. In R, it inverts the cost: instead of fewer reallocations, you get larger per-write copies forever. The right default for an append-only buffer is small initial allocation plus grow-by-doubling, exactly like in compiled languages — but for *different* reasons.

## The legacy cleanup (Batch 1)

While the buffer was the operational headline, the legacy cleanup was the one that *enabled* everything else. The optimizer's job is much harder when it has to preserve obsolete invariants. The cycle deliberately did the cleanup first.

Three pre-snapshot, pre-function-strategy execution surfaces were removed:

### 1. The raw `bars` execution path

ledgr originally (v0.1.0) accepted runs against a raw, mutable `bars` table in a DuckDB file. v0.1.1 introduced sealed snapshots — hash-verified, immutable bar packs — and modern ledgr execution runs against those.

The raw `bars` path survived as a compatibility surface. It bypassed the snapshot hash verification, the coverage check, and the OHLC-validity check. It existed for a v0.1.0 audience that no longer exists.

We removed it. Now every execution entry (`ledgr_run()`, `ledgr_sweep()`, `ledgr_backtest()`, and the low-level `ledgr_backtest_run()`) fails loudly *before* the fold begins if the config isn't sealed-snapshot-backed. The user-facing `ledgr_backtest(snapshot = a_data_frame)` convenience still works, but it converts the data frame into a sealed snapshot before doing anything else.

### 2. The run-time data-subset value hash

ledgr was computing, on every run, a SHA-256 hash of every bar value the run touched (`ledgr_run_data_subset_hash`). Its job was drift detection: "did the data the run depends on change between the original run and a resume attempt?"

But ledgr now only runs against sealed snapshots. Sealed snapshots are immutable and already carry their own `snapshot_hash`. So the data physically *cannot* drift — the value hash was re-deriving a guarantee the snapshot seal already provides.

It was also expensive: the per-value `formatC(round(x, 8), format = "f", digits = 8)` canonicalization was about **14.7% of the empty-fold profile** — one of the largest single contributors in the no-trade case.

We removed it. Resume identity for sealed runs is now `(snapshot_hash, ordered instrument_ids, inclusive start_ts/end_ts)` — a cheap composite that's already trustworthy because the snapshot is.

### 3. R6 strategy support

ledgr originally supported two kinds of user strategies:

- Plain functions: `function(ctx, params) -> targets` (one execution path).
- R6 class objects with an `$on_pulse(ctx)` method (a different execution path, with a runtime mutation guard).

Two paths created drift. The R6 mutation guard fired on replay but not on direct runs (an inconsistency that was caught during the cleanup). R6 strategies and function strategies took slightly different routes through the runner. Replay equality wasn't byte-perfect across paths.

We removed R6 strategy support. All four built-in reference strategies (`hold_zero`, `echo`, `ts_rule`, `state_prev`) were rewritten as plain functions. The runner has one strategy invocation path now. The `R6` package was removed from imports.

### The dependency cleanup that came with it

Batch 2 followed up: `cli` was dropped (it had been imported and never used in production code), `R6` was dropped (per above), and `collapse` was added — a pure-C R package with **zero transitive dependencies**, intended as a hot-path optimization tool. Net import count: 9 → 8.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    Before["BEFORE: 9 imports<br/>(cli, R6, collapse-not-yet,<br/>+ 8 core)"]
    After["AFTER: 8 imports<br/>(collapse + 7 core,<br/>cli & R6 removed)"]
    Before --> After
```

## The representation cleanup (Batch 5, Lane R + Lane A)

After the buffer rock came off, the next-biggest costs in the profile were timestamp and string formatting — `format.POSIXlt`, `sprintf`, `paste`, and `formatC`. The "empty-fold" profile (a backtest with no trades and no features) was dominated by formatting work, even though there were literally no events to write.

The cause was a pervasive habit: a value would arrive as `POSIXct`, get formatted to an ISO string for some intermediate purpose, then get parsed back to `POSIXct` for the next layer, then formatted again for the layer after that. Each round-trip was visible as `format.POSIXlt` time in the profile.

### What we did

The cleanup has two sides:

**Lane R: carry typed values through hot paths, format only at validated boundaries.** We added a small set of helpers in `R/timestamp.R`:

- `ledgr_assert_whole_second_utc(x)` — validates that a `POSIXct` is whole-second UTC; aborts otherwise.
- `ledgr_ts_utc_posix(x)` — the polymorphic gateway. If the input is already `POSIXct`, validate and pass through. If it's a character ISO string (legacy/external input), normalize through the existing parser. Either way, return a trusted `POSIXct`.

The per-fill payload, the fill-model proposal, and the ledger writer all went from this pattern:

```r
ts_iso   <- ledgr_normalize_ts_utc(ts_in)              # format()
ts_posix <- as.POSIXct(ts_iso, format = "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ", tz = "UTC")
# ...use ts_posix...
```

to this:

```r
ts_posix <- ledgr_ts_utc_posix(ts_in)   # trusts POSIXct, normalizes character
# ...use ts_posix...
```

The new path is byte-identical to the old one for both `POSIXct` and character inputs, but skips a `format()` + `parse()` round-trip on every fill.

**Lane A: do run-level work once, not per loop iteration.** The session-local feature cache was constructing its lookup key by:

1. Normalizing `start_ts_utc` and `end_ts_utc` (a `format()` call each).
2. Serializing a JSON payload.
3. Hashing it with SHA-256.

This happened **inside** the per-instrument, per-feature loop. For a workload with 100 instruments × 20 features, that's 2,000 redundant timestamp normalizations and 2,000 SHA-256 hashes per run.

We hoisted the timestamp normalization to run scope (one call instead of 2,000) and replaced the JSON+SHA encoding with a length-prefixed composite string. The new encoding is much cheaper and is collision-free because the length prefix prevents delimiter ambiguity:

```text
ledgr_feature_cache_v2|<n>:<val>|<n>:<val>|...
```

It's important that this cache key is **session-local in-memory only** — not part of any durable identity. We verified this against the codebase before changing it, because Lane R has a hard rule that durable identity bytes (`canonical_json`, snapshot hashes, feature fingerprints, config hashes) are fenced and cannot change.

### Sub-second timestamps are now rejected at the seal

While we were touching the timestamp path, we made the whole-second contract explicit and enforced. ledgr is not an HFT engine; sub-second precision is out of scope. Sub-second input is now **rejected** at snapshot ingest (in `ledgr_snapshot_from_df`) and at seal validation (a DuckDB-level check in `ledgr_snapshot_validate_for_seal`), rather than silently truncated. Defense in depth: two layers, both fail loud.

### What we measured

On the same matched 500×1,260 SMA crossover workload:

| Phase | Post-B0 baseline | Post-Batch-5 | Δ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Wall | 32.91 s | 31.25 s | **−5.0%** |
| t_pre (setup) | 1.50 s | 1.11 s | **−26%** (Lane A) |
| t_loop | 20.87 s | 19.42 s | **−7.0%** (Lane R) |

The proportional `t_pre` win (−26%) is what Lane A delivers when the per-iteration normalization is hoisted out. The loop win (−7%) is Lane R reducing per-event timestamp work. Both are within the synthesis's pre-implementation predicted bounds.

The honest framing: the matched workload has only two features and is turnover-dominated, so Lane R/A's gains are modest. Their big wins will land on low-turnover, wide-universe, and intraday workloads, where per-pulse representation cost scales with the pulse count.

## The reconstruction cleanup (Batch 6, Lane C)

ledgr stores events; results are reconstructed from those events. `ledgr_fills_from_events()` is the function that walks an event stream and produces the user-facing "fills" table.

### The anti-pattern that was there

The reconstruction loop was building one tiny `data.frame` per output row and assembling them at the end with `do.call(rbind, list_of_dataframes)`:

```r
rows <- list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(events))) {
  # ...compute close_qty, open_qty, etc...
  rows[[length(rows) + 1L]] <- data.frame(
    event_seq    = ...,
    ts_utc       = ...,
    instrument_id = ...,
    # ...nine columns...
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}
result <- do.call(rbind, rows)
```

This is the single most common R performance anti-pattern. Both halves of it are slow:

- **`data.frame(...)` per row** allocates a tiny data.frame with row.names attributes, class dispatch overhead, etc.
- **`do.call(rbind, list_of_dataframes)`** is *quadratic*: each `rbind` recopies the accumulating result, so N rbinds end up doing O(N²) work.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE: list-of-data.frames + rbind (O(N²))"]
        L1["row 1: tiny data.frame"]
        L2["row 2: tiny data.frame"]
        L3["row 3: tiny data.frame"]
        LDots["..."]
        Rbind["do.call(rbind, ...)<br/>recopies on every step"]
        L1 --> Rbind
        L2 --> Rbind
        L3 --> Rbind
        LDots --> Rbind
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER: preallocated primitive columns (O(N))"]
        Cols["typed column vectors<br/>(integer, POSIXct, character, numeric)"]
        Fill["fill in row N by index"]
        Materialize["data.frame(col1=..., col2=...)<br/>built once at the end"]
        Cols --> Fill --> Materialize
    end
    style BEFORE fill:#fff0f0
    style AFTER fill:#f0fff0
```

### The fix

We added a primitive-column buffer in `R/fold-core.R`, structurally similar to B0's buffer but for fill rows. Each output column is its own typed vector (preallocated to roughly `2 × nrow(events)` to accommodate close-then-open splits, with grow-by-doubling as fallback). The reconstruction loop fills the columns by index. At the end, a single `data.frame(col1 = col1_vec, col2 = col2_vec, ...)` call materializes the final result.

We also used `.subset2(events, "col")` (a primitive S3-bypass accessor) instead of `events$col` or `events[i, "col"]` inside the hot loop, which avoids `$.data.frame`/`[.data.frame` dispatch overhead on every read.

The same buffer machinery now serves both:

- `ledgr_fills_from_events()` (in-memory reconstruction).
- `ledgr_extract_fills_impl()` (the DuckDB-backed chunked reader).

Both code paths share the helper, so they cannot drift.

### What we measured

Synthetic reconstruction timing for a 13,355-event ledger (the same scale as the matched-peer workload):

| Metric | Before | After |
|---|---:|---:|
| Median reconstruction time | 8.27 s | 4.92 s |
| Speedup | — | **1.68×** |

A couple of things worth noting honestly:

- This is **read-back / materialization time**, not run-wall time. The reconstruction happens when the user calls `ledgr_results(bt, "fills")`, not during the live backtest loop.
- The reconstruction spike (`spike-reconstruction-collapse.md`) had measured `collapse::rowbind` at **58×** the old pattern. We chose base R for this batch — the structural fix without taking on a `collapse` value-bearing surface — and got 1.68×. The `collapse::rowbind` lever is still available behind the determinism wrapper if reconstruction ever becomes a hot spot.

The conservative choice is intentional. Adding a value-bearing `collapse` operation here would have meant relying on the Batch 4 wrapper plus parity fixtures across CASHFLOW-before-fill, partial close/open, split rows, invalid rows, DB-backed and memory-backed parity, etc. The base-R rewrite gets a real win without expanding the parity surface.

## The collapse determinism gate (Batch 4)

This is small in code but conceptually important.

### Why it exists

The `collapse` package is a pure-C R library that ships fast replacements for many common operations (`fmean`, `fsd`, `fcumsum`, `rowbind`, and so on). Some of its functions have **option-state-dependent behavior**: they read global options set by `collapse::set_collapse()` to decide things like whether to remove NAs, whether to sort grouped results, how many threads to use, and whether to use stable algorithms.

That's fine for interactive use. For ledgr, where the correctness guarantee is "identical inputs produce byte-identical outputs," it's a problem. If a user (or an unrelated package) calls `set_collapse(na.rm = TRUE, nthreads = 2)` somewhere in their session before running a backtest, a value-bearing collapse operation inside ledgr could silently return a different result. That breaks reproducibility.

### What we did

We added an internal wrapper, `ledgr_with_collapse_deterministic(expr)`, that wraps any code which uses value-bearing collapse operations. The wrapper:

1. Captures the caller's full current `set_collapse()` state.
2. Registers a restore for that state on exit (normal *or* error) via `on.exit`.
3. Applies a known deterministic state (all 9 host-exposed fields pinned: `nthreads = 1L`, `na.rm = FALSE`, `sort = TRUE`, `stable.algo = TRUE`, plus the others).
4. Evaluates the expression in the caller's frame against the pinned state.
5. Lets `on.exit` restore the caller's state when the expression returns or errors.

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller
    participant Wrapper as ledgr_with_collapse_deterministic
    participant Collapse as collapse::set_collapse
    participant Expr as wrapped expression

    Caller->>Wrapper: invoke(expr)
    Wrapper->>Collapse: capture caller state
    Note right of Wrapper: register on.exit(restore)
    Wrapper->>Collapse: apply pinned deterministic state
    Wrapper->>Expr: evaluate (against deterministic state)
    Expr-->>Wrapper: result OR error
    Note right of Wrapper: on.exit fires
    Wrapper->>Collapse: restore caller state
    Wrapper-->>Caller: return result (or propagate error)
```

The hostile-setting test fixture proves this works: with `na.rm = TRUE` set in hostile caller state, `collapse::fmean(c(1, NA, 3))` returns `2` *unwrapped* but `NA` *wrapped*. The wrapper neutralizes the hostile state without changing what the operation conceptually does.

### Why the wrapper had to exist before any value-bearing collapse use

The gate is a contract. Before this wrapper lands, no code in ledgr may use `collapse::fmean`, `fcumsum`, `rowbind`, or any other value-bearing collapse operation in production, even if it would be faster. Lane C (reconstruction) explicitly waited for this wrapper to exist before being allowed to consider `collapse::rowbind`. The wrapper is the reason that future optimization-with-`collapse` work is *possible* without breaking the reproducibility USP.

It's worth noting: ledgr currently doesn't *use* the wrapper anywhere in production code. It exists, it's tested, it's correct. It will start to matter the first time a reconstruction or metrics rewrite reaches for `collapse::rowbind` or `collapse::fcumsum`.

## The result, fairly stated

Same-host peer comparison, same workload (500 symbols × 1,260 daily bars, SMA crossover), same power profile:

| Engine | Path | Wall (s) | Bars/sec | Fills |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Backtrader | Python `bt.indicators.SimpleMovingAverage` | 64.69 | 9,739 | n/a |
| ledgr | ledgr built-in SMA (pure R `ledgr_rolling_mean`) | 86.02 | 7,324 | 13,355 |
| **ledgr** | **TTR-backed SMA (C-accelerated)** | **33.28** | **18,930** | **13,355** |
| quantstrat | TTR-backed SMA + xts blotter | 110.87 | 5,682 | 12,787 |

Three things to read honestly out of this table:

1. **With the TTR-backed SMA path, ledgr's engine is faster than Backtrader's by about 1.94×, on a workload that's idiomatic for each engine.** That's a real, defensible engine-comparison number.
2. **With ledgr's built-in SMA path, ledgr is slower than Backtrader.** That's not an engine issue; it's an indicator-implementation issue. ledgr's built-in `ledgr_ind_sma()` is a pure-R rolling mean. TTR's `SMA` is C. The engine work in this cycle didn't address that disparity — it's a separate (and real) question about the canonical fast indicator path for ledgr users.
3. **ledgr does this while writing a sealed, deterministic event ledger to DuckDB on every fill.** Backtrader and quantstrat hold their blotter in process memory and discard it at the end of the session. ledgr writes events with per-row canonical JSON metadata, snapshot hash verification, replay-byte preservation. The peers don't pay that correctness tax. ledgr now wins the speed race while doing more correctness work per bar.

In context: ledgr was *abysmally slow* a couple of months ago. The cycle moved it from "behind everything interpreted" to "best-in-class among interpreted R/Python event-driven backtesters, on this workload, with reproducibility built in." Compiled engines (LEAN's C#, vectorbt's vectorized model) are still about an order of magnitude faster, but that's a language gap, not an architecture gap.

## Lessons learned about R and package development

Some of these are specific to ledgr; most generalize.

### 1. In R, "preallocate to be safe" is often actively harmful

R's copy-on-modify semantics mean that `vec[[i]] <- value` on a refcount-elevated vector copies the entire vector. The bigger the vector, the more expensive the per-write copy. So preallocating to a worst-case size — a habit borrowed from compiled languages where in-place writes are stable — converts an O(N) total cost into an O(N × buffer_size) cost.

The right pattern for append-only buffers in R is the same one C++ and Rust use for dynamic arrays: start small, grow by doubling. The amortized per-write cost is O(1).

### 2. The list-of-data.frames + `do.call(rbind, ...)` anti-pattern is real and easy to spot

If a function constructs `data.frame()` calls inside a loop and concatenates them with `do.call(rbind, ...)` at the end, it has at least quadratic cost in the number of rows. The reliable cure is:

1. Preallocate one typed vector per column.
2. Fill the vectors by index inside the loop.
3. Build the final `data.frame(col1 = col1_vec, col2 = col2_vec, ...)` once.

This pattern shows up dozens of times in R packages in the wild. Grepping for it is a high-yield audit.

### 3. `format.POSIXlt` is surprisingly expensive

R's POSIXct-to-string formatting (`format()` on a `POSIXct`, which dispatches to `format.POSIXlt`) is among the slowest single operations in many R profiles. If you can carry typed `POSIXct` values through hot paths and only format at validated boundaries (ingestion, durable output), the savings are substantial — especially on workloads with high pulse counts.

### 4. `.subset2(df, "col")` beats `$` or `[[` for hot-loop column reads

`$.data.frame` and `[[.data.frame` go through S3 dispatch. `.subset2()` is a primitive that skips dispatch and returns the column directly. Inside a tight reconstruction loop with millions of iterations, the difference adds up.

### 5. R6 introduces hidden costs and reproducibility fragility

R6 classes carry per-instance environments, method dispatch overhead, and (in ledgr's case) a runtime mutation guard whose semantics didn't align cleanly with replay byte-identity. Plain functions with explicit state (state in / state out) tend to be faster, simpler, and easier to make reproducible.

This isn't a blanket condemnation of R6 — there are uses where reference semantics are exactly what's wanted. But for an engine whose correctness guarantee is byte-identical replay, the function-with-explicit-state model is usually the better fit.

### 6. Global option-state in dependencies is a reproducibility hazard

Any R package whose functions read global options (`collapse::set_collapse`, `options(digits = ...)`, locale settings, etc.) is potentially a reproducibility hazard in a deterministic system. The deterministic-wrapper pattern — capture caller state, register `on.exit(restore)`, apply pinned state, evaluate, restore — is the general fix. It's worth a tiny helper in any package that depends on option-state-reading libraries.

### 7. DuckDB is fast enough — the wrapper code around it usually isn't

A surprising amount of "ledgr is slow at writing to DuckDB" turned out to be R-level wrapping cost (the over-allocated buffer, the per-row data.frame construction), not DuckDB itself. DuckDB's columnar C engine sustains high write throughput; the R code marshalling data into it was the bottleneck. Profile the R wrapping first; the database is usually fine.

### 8. Spike → real-run re-profile is the only honest discipline

Isolated micro-benchmarks lie. They run on synthesized data with idealized refcount conditions that don't match production. A spike is useful for establishing *mechanism* — does this technique work at all, and what's the rough magnitude? — but the wall verdict is always the production re-profile. Several times during this cycle, spike results would have been wildly misleading without that follow-up step.

### 9. Amdahl is non-negotiable

A 100× speedup on a component that's 5% of the wall is at most a 1.05× wall speedup. Modeling the expected wall effect *before* implementation prevents two failure modes: over-promising in changelogs, and over-investing in optimizations whose Amdahl bound is too small to justify the parity-gate work.

### 10. "Pre-CRAN, no users" is an architectural lever, not just a convenience

Most of the cleanup in this cycle would have been impossibly expensive with users in the field — the data-subset value hash change, the R6 strategy removal, the raw-`bars` removal would each have required deprecation cycles, dual-path support, migration tooling. Pre-CRAN status meant we could just delete them.

That's a one-shot opportunity. Aggressive cleanup *before* the first CRAN release is the right time to do it, even at the cost of feeling "destabilizing." Every removable surface that survives to CRAN is a long-term maintenance liability.

### 11. Measurement discipline produces better engineering than enthusiasm does

It's tempting to ship a "10× wall speedup" claim when the wall drops from 313s to 33s. We didn't, because the local CPU power profile had changed between the two measurements. The honest claim was "buffer share of R time dropped from 72% to 3.5% on current source" — a within-run, apples-to-apples number.

This discipline is uncomfortable in the moment (you sound less impressive) but pays off in the long run. The numbers in this writeup are claims we can defend if challenged.

## Recommendations for the future

### Near-term

1. **Decide the canonical fast indicator path.** ledgr's TTR-backed numbers are excellent. Its built-in `ledgr_ind_sma()` is pure-R rolling mean and gives away the win on workloads where users haven't reached for TTR. Options: (a) promote TTR from Suggests to Imports; (b) replace the built-ins with C-accelerated implementations; (c) document the `series_fn` vectorized pattern as the canonical fast path and steer users explicitly. The integration story with packages like `ta-lib-R` is worth opening as part of this.

2. **Parallel sweep dispatch (v0.1.8.8).** ledgr's sweep mode was structurally designed for parallelism — per-candidate ephemeral output and a deterministic reproduction key. DuckDB supports concurrent reads from multiple processes. The work is plausibly straightforward now that the single-core path is clean.

### Medium-term

3. **A compiled fold core (C, Rcpp, or Rust).** Interpreted-runtime backtesters top out around 20–50k bars/sec because each pulse pays the R interpreter's per-iteration cost. Compiled engines (LEAN, vectorbt) are an order of magnitude faster. ledgr's architecture — sealed snapshots, events as truth, function-strategy contract — is preserved across a language port; what would migrate is the fold loop itself. This is the v0.2.x lever for LEAN-class performance.

4. **Ragged-universe / asset-lifetime support.** Realistic backtesting universes have IPOs, delistings, halts, and exchange holidays — a security legitimately doesn't have a bar at every pulse. ledgr's current "complete panel required" stance is correct for the reproducibility USP but limits realistic-universe research. The mainstream multi-asset backtesters (Zipline, LEAN) handle this via asset lifetimes plus forward-fill; ledgr would want to do it with an *explicit, sealed* absence/imputation policy (not silent ffill, which would break the reproducibility model). A real RFC.

5. **Persisted sweep mode (now viable thanks to B0).** Pre-cycle, durable per-candidate writes were prohibitive at sweep scale. Post-B0 they're tractable for moderate N. There's a real workflow where users want every candidate's full ledger written durably — debugging strategy logic candidate-by-candidate, comparing decision trails, drawing conclusions from per-candidate event streams. A first-class execution mode for this is worth thinking through.

6. **Intraday minute/hourly support.** Now viable from a throughput standpoint — 50M bars (500 symbols × 1 year of minute data) is about 44 minutes on the current engine. Gating items: ragged-universe support (above), feature-width memory discipline, and the canonical fast indicator path (item 1). Worth scoping when those land.

### Longer-term

7. **The deterministic wrapper pattern, formalized.** Other packages will arrive with option-state-reading functions. A small public/internal documentation note explaining the wrapper pattern — and ledgr's choice to apply it whenever a value-bearing operation reads global options — would be useful both as a discipline document and as guidance for any future contributor.

8. **A perf-regression test in CI.** The cycle's measurement discipline depended on humans running benchmarks deliberately. A small, automated post-merge perf check (durable run on a fixed workload, posted to a tracking file) would catch regressions cheaply. The infrastructure for this is mostly in place via `dev/bench/run_benchmarks.R`.

## Closing

The cycle's arc is, in one sentence: **disciplined measurement plus aggressive legacy removal plus targeted hot-path fixes turned ledgr from "the slow one" into "the fast one in its class," while preserving and even strengthening the reproducibility guarantees that justify the project's existence.**

The single highest-leverage change was technical-but-pedestrian: replace worst-case preallocation with grow-by-doubling. That single decision moved more wall time than every other change in the cycle combined.

The most generalizable lessons aren't about ledgr at all. They're about R: the over-allocation trap, the list-of-data.frames anti-pattern, the cost of `format.POSIXlt`, the importance of carrying typed values, the discipline of "measure, don't guess." Any R package author working on performance-sensitive code can take those forward without ever looking at ledgr's source.

And the most important meta-lesson is that an honest profile is worth more than a brilliant guess. Before this cycle, we believed ledgr was slow for a hundred speculated reasons. The profile said: it was slow for one reason. We fixed that one reason. Almost everything else fell into place because we had been carrying it for company, not because it was actually doing work.
